name: Metoprolol
primaryEffect: Reduces heart rate and myocardial contractility
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, angina, heart failure (HFrEF), post-MI, atrial fibrillation rate control, SVT prevention
mechanismOfAction: Selectively antagonizes β1-adrenergic receptors in the heart, reducing sympathetic stimulation of heart rate, contractility, and renin release.
sideEffects: Bradycardia, fatigue, cold extremities, depression, sexual dysfunction, masks hypoglycemia symptoms, bronchospasm (less than non-selective), rebound hypertension on abrupt discontinuation
contraindications: Cardiogenic shock, decompensated heart failure, significant bradycardia or AV block without pacemaker, severe reactive airway disease
tooltip: Cardioselective β1-blocker reducing heart rate, contractility, and renin release. High-yield for hypertension, angina, HFrEF, and rate control. Classic USMLE associations: masks hypoglycemia, causes bronchospasm, rebound hypertension if stopped abruptly.

name: Atenolol
primaryEffect: Reduces heart rate and blood pressure with minimal CNS penetration
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, angina, post-MI, atrial fibrillation rate control
mechanismOfAction: Selectively antagonizes β1-adrenergic receptors; hydrophilic so poor CNS penetration and less likely to cause fatigue or nightmares than propranolol.
sideEffects: Bradycardia, fatigue, cold extremities, masks hypoglycemia, less CNS effects than lipophilic beta-blockers, avoid in renal failure (renally excreted)
contraindications: Cardiogenic shock, significant bradycardia or AV block without pacemaker, decompensated heart failure, severe reactive airway disease
tooltip: Cardioselective β1-blocker. Hydrophilic — less CNS penetration than propranolol (fewer nightmares, fatigue). Renally excreted — dose-reduce in CKD. Masks hypoglycemia. USMLE Step 1 classic for post-MI secondary prevention.

name: Carvedilol
primaryEffect: Reduces heart rate, contractility, and afterload via combined β and α1 blockade
treatmentUsage: Heart failure (HFrEF), hypertension, post-MI left ventricular dysfunction
mechanismOfAction: Non-selective β1 and β2 antagonist plus α1-adrenergic receptor blocker, causing vasodilation and reducing preload and afterload in addition to rate/contractility reduction.
sideEffects: Orthostatic hypotension (from α1 block), bradycardia, dizziness, fatigue, bronchospasm, masks hypoglycemia
contraindications: Decompensated heart failure, cardiogenic shock, significant bradycardia or AV block, severe reactive airway disease
tooltip: Non-selective β + α1 blocker. Preferred in HFrEF — reduces mortality. Orthostatic hypotension is the key side effect from α1 blockade. Bronchospasm risk from β2 blockade. Key distinction from metoprolol: vasodilation via α1.

name: Propranolol
primaryEffect: Non-selective β blockade reducing heart rate, contractility, and renin release
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, angina, arrhythmias, essential tremor, migraine prophylaxis, hyperthyroidism symptoms, portal hypertension, performance anxiety
mechanismOfAction: Non-selectively antagonizes both β1 and β2 adrenergic receptors; lipophilic — crosses blood-brain barrier, explaining CNS effects and efficacy in tremor/migraine.
sideEffects: Bradycardia, bronchospasm (significant — β2 block), fatigue, depression, vivid dreams/nightmares, masks hypoglycemia, sexual dysfunction, cold extremities
contraindications: Asthma or COPD, cardiogenic shock, bradycardia or AV block, decompensated heart failure, cocaine toxicity (leaves α-receptors unopposed)
tooltip: Non-selective β1+β2 blocker. Lipophilic — crosses BBB (useful for tremor, migraine, anxiety). Significant bronchospasm risk. Never use in cocaine toxicity (unopposed α causes hypertensive crisis). Also used for hyperthyroid symptoms and portal HTN.

name: Labetalol
primaryEffect: Reduces blood pressure via combined β and α1 blockade without reflex tachycardia
treatmentUsage: Hypertensive emergency (especially in pregnancy), hypertension in pregnancy (preeclampsia), pheochromocytoma crisis management
mechanismOfAction: Blocks β1, β2, and α1-adrenergic receptors. The α1 blockade provides vasodilation while β blockade prevents reflex tachycardia, making it uniquely effective for hypertensive emergencies.
sideEffects: Orthostatic hypotension, bradycardia, bronchospasm, fatigue, hepatotoxicity (rare)
contraindications: Asthma, significant bradycardia or AV block, cardiogenic shock, decompensated heart failure
tooltip: α1 + β blocker — first-line IV for hypertensive emergency in pregnancy. Prevents reflex tachycardia (key advantage). Safe in preeclampsia. Avoid in asthma. Classic USMLE scenario: pregnant patient with severe hypertension.

name: Esmolol
primaryEffect: Ultra-short-acting heart rate reduction via β1 blockade
treatmentUsage: Acute rate control in atrial fibrillation/flutter, supraventricular tachycardia, perioperative hypertension and tachycardia, aortic dissection
mechanismOfAction: Selective β1 antagonist with extremely short half-life (~9 minutes) due to ester hydrolysis by red blood cell esterases; allows rapid titration and termination.
sideEffects: Hypotension, bradycardia, transient nausea; effects dissipate within minutes of stopping infusion
contraindications: Severe bradycardia, AV block, cardiogenic shock, decompensated heart failure
tooltip: Ultra-short-acting IV β1-blocker (half-life ~9 min). Ideal for acute settings requiring rapid, titratable rate control. Metabolized by RBC esterases — works even in liver/renal failure. Used in aortic dissection to reduce HR and BP.

name: Lisinopril
primaryEffect: Reduces blood pressure and afterload by blocking angiotensin II production
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, heart failure (HFrEF), post-MI, diabetic nephropathy, CKD with proteinuria
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE), preventing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II; reduces aldosterone, causing vasodilation and natriuresis.
sideEffects: Dry cough (bradykinin accumulation — most common reason for discontinuation), angioedema (potentially life-threatening), hyperkalemia, first-dose hypotension, acute kidney injury in bilateral renal artery stenosis, teratogenic (category D/X)
contraindications: Pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, prior angioedema from ACE inhibitor, concurrent use with sacubitril/valsartan or aliskiren
tooltip: ACE inhibitor — blocks conversion of angiotensin I → II. Classic USMLE side effects: dry cough (bradykinin), angioedema (switch to ARB). Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Renoprotective in diabetics. Use cautiously in bilateral renal artery stenosis.

name: Enalapril
primaryEffect: Reduces blood pressure and afterload; prodrug requiring hepatic activation
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, heart failure (HFrEF), post-MI, diabetic nephropathy
mechanismOfAction: Prodrug converted to enalaprilat in the liver; inhibits ACE, decreasing angiotensin II and aldosterone, causing vasodilation and mild natriuresis.
sideEffects: Dry cough, angioedema, hyperkalemia, first-dose hypotension, teratogenic, acute kidney injury in bilateral renal artery stenosis
contraindications: Pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, prior ACE inhibitor angioedema, hepatic failure (impaired activation)
tooltip: ACE inhibitor prodrug — activated in the liver to enalaprilat. Same class effects as lisinopril: dry cough, angioedema, teratogen. Contraindicated in hepatic failure (can't activate). Proven mortality benefit in HFrEF.

name: Captopril
primaryEffect: Reduces blood pressure; first ACE inhibitor developed, contains sulfhydryl group
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, heart failure, post-MI, diabetic nephropathy, scleroderma renal crisis
mechanismOfAction: Directly inhibits ACE via its sulfhydryl group (not a prodrug); reduces angiotensin II and aldosterone, causing vasodilation and reduced sodium retention.
sideEffects: Dry cough, angioedema, hyperkalemia, rash, dysgeusia (loss of taste — from sulfhydryl group), agranulocytosis (rare, especially in renal impairment), neutropenia
contraindications: Pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, prior ACE inhibitor angioedema
tooltip: First ACE inhibitor. Contains sulfhydryl group — causes rash, dysgeusia, and agranulocytosis (unique to captopril). Not a prodrug. Used in scleroderma renal crisis. All ACE inhibitor class effects: cough, angioedema, teratogen, hyperkalemia.

name: Ramipril
primaryEffect: Reduces blood pressure and demonstrated mortality reduction post-MI
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, heart failure, post-MI (reduces mortality — HOPE trial), diabetic nephropathy, high cardiovascular risk patients
mechanismOfAction: Prodrug converted to ramiprilat; inhibits ACE, reduces angiotensin II and aldosterone. The HOPE trial demonstrated cardiovascular event reduction beyond blood pressure lowering.
sideEffects: Dry cough, angioedema, hyperkalemia, first-dose hypotension, teratogenic
contraindications: Pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, prior ACE inhibitor angioedema
tooltip: ACE inhibitor with landmark HOPE trial data — reduced MI, stroke, and CV death in high-risk patients beyond BP control. Prodrug. Standard ACE side effects: cough, angioedema, hyperkalemia, teratogen.

name: Losartan
primaryEffect: Blocks angiotensin II at AT1 receptor, reducing blood pressure without causing cough
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, heart failure (HFrEF), diabetic nephropathy (type 2 DM), post-MI, hyperuricemia/gout (unique uricosuric effect)
mechanismOfAction: Selectively blocks AT1 angiotensin II receptors, preventing vasoconstriction and aldosterone release without affecting ACE — thus bradykinin does not accumulate and no cough occurs.
sideEffects: Hyperkalemia, first-dose hypotension, teratogenic, acute kidney injury in bilateral renal artery stenosis; NO cough (key difference from ACE inhibitors)
contraindications: Pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, concurrent use with ACE inhibitor in diabetics, concurrent use with aliskiren
tooltip: ARB — blocks AT1 receptor. Used when ACE inhibitors cause intolerable cough. NO bradykinin accumulation = no cough. Still causes angioedema (rare) and hyperkalemia. Unique property: uricosuric (lowers uric acid) — useful in patients with gout and hypertension.

name: Valsartan
primaryEffect: Blocks angiotensin II at AT1 receptor, reducing blood pressure and cardiac remodeling
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, heart failure (HFrEF), post-MI with LV dysfunction, also component of sacubitril/valsartan (ARNI)
mechanismOfAction: Selectively blocks AT1 angiotensin II receptors, reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and cardiac/vascular remodeling.
sideEffects: Hyperkalemia, first-dose hypotension, teratogenic, dizziness; NO cough
contraindications: Pregnancy, bilateral renal artery stenosis, concurrent ACE inhibitor use (avoid dual RAAS blockade except in specific HF protocols)
tooltip: ARB used in HFrEF (as standalone or in sacubitril/valsartan). Blocks AT1 receptor — no cough, but still hyperkalemia and teratogenicity. Do NOT combine with ACE inhibitor (dual RAAS blockade increases harm).

name: Amlodipine
primaryEffect: Vasodilation via L-type calcium channel blockade in vascular smooth muscle
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, stable angina, vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina, Raynaud phenomenon
mechanismOfAction: Blocks L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, causing vasodilation and reduced afterload. Minimal cardiac conduction effects (DHP selectivity).
sideEffects: Peripheral edema (ankle swelling — most common complaint), flushing, headache, reflex tachycardia, gingival hyperplasia
contraindications: Cardiogenic shock, severe aortic stenosis (relative), do not use for acute decompensated heart failure
tooltip: Long-acting DHP calcium channel blocker — peripheral vasodilation. Most prescribed antihypertensive. Key side effect: peripheral edema (not from fluid overload — add ACE inhibitor to reduce it). Safe in angina. Minimal effect on heart rate.

name: Nifedipine
primaryEffect: Arterial vasodilation; reflex tachycardia is pronounced
treatmentUsage: Hypertension, vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina, Raynaud phenomenon, preterm labor (tocolysis), hypertensive urgency (oral form)
mechanismOfAction: Blocks L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle; short-acting formulations cause abrupt vasodilation and pronounced reflex sympathetic activation and tachycardia.
sideEffects: Significant reflex tachycardia (especially short-acting), peripheral edema, flushing, headache; short-acting sublingual form associated with increased MI risk (avoid)
contraindications: Avoid sublingual short-acting form (causes erratic absorption and dangerous hypotension), significant aortic stenosis
tooltip: DHP CCB with pronounced reflex tachycardia — especially the short-acting form (avoid sublingual — dangerous, associated with MI). Used for Prinzmetal angina, Raynaud, and preterm labor (tocolysis). USMLE: distinguish from amlodipine by stronger reflex tachycardia.

name: Diltiazem
primaryEffect: Reduces heart rate and AV conduction in addition to mild vasodilation
treatmentUsage: Atrial fibrillation/flutter rate control, SVT, vasospastic angina, stable angina, hypertension
mechanismOfAction: Blocks L-type calcium channels in both cardiac tissue (SA/AV node slowing) and vascular smooth muscle; intermediate between verapamil (most cardiac) and amlodipine (most vascular).
sideEffects: Bradycardia, AV block, constipation, hypotension, peripheral edema, headache; negative inotropy (avoid in HFrEF)
contraindications: HFrEF (negative inotropic effect), sick sinus syndrome or AV block without pacemaker, concurrent use with beta-blockers (risk of complete heart block), WPW syndrome
tooltip: Non-DHP CCB — rate control AND vasodilation. Key USMLE: do NOT use in HFrEF (negative inotropy worsens pump failure). Do NOT combine with beta-blockers (complete heart block risk). Used for AF rate control and angina.

name: Verapamil
primaryEffect: Most potent negative inotrope and chronotrope among CCBs
treatmentUsage: Atrial fibrillation/flutter rate control, SVT (including AVNRT), stable angina, hypertension, cluster headache prophylaxis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
mechanismOfAction: Blocks L-type calcium channels predominantly in cardiac tissue (SA node, AV node, myocardium) with less vascular selectivity than diltiazem; most negative inotropic CCB.
sideEffects: Bradycardia, AV block, constipation (most common side effect — most constipating CCB), hypotension, significant negative inotropy
contraindications: HFrEF, sick sinus syndrome or AV block without pacemaker, concurrent beta-blockers, WPW syndrome (can accelerate conduction via accessory pathway), ventricular tachycardia
tooltip: Most negatively inotropic and chronotropic CCB. Classic USMLE: constipation (most constipating CCB), contraindicated in HFrEF and WPW. Do NOT combine with beta-blockers. Used for SVT, AF rate control, cluster headache prophylaxis.

name: Furosemide
primaryEffect: Potent diuresis via inhibition of Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in thick ascending Loop of Henle
treatmentUsage: Acute pulmonary edema, chronic heart failure volume overload, edema, hypertension (less preferred), hypercalcemia, acute kidney injury (oliguric phase)
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits the Na⁺/K⁺/2Cl⁻ cotransporter (NKCC2) in the thick ascending limb of Henle's loop, preventing reabsorption of ~25% of filtered sodium and causing significant diuresis.
sideEffects: Hypokalemia (most common), hypomagnesemia, hyponatremia, hyperuricemia (gout attacks), metabolic alkalosis, ototoxicity (especially IV, with aminoglycosides), dehydration, sulfa allergy cross-reactivity
contraindications: Sulfa allergy (use ethacrynic acid instead), anuria, severe hypokalemia or hyponatremia
tooltip: Most potent diuretic class. Inhibits NKCC2 in thick ascending limb. Key side effects: hypokalemia, ototoxicity (especially with aminoglycosides), hyperuricemia. Sulfa allergy — use ethacrynic acid instead. First-line for acute pulmonary edema.

name: Bumetanide
primaryEffect: Potent diuresis; more potent per mg than furosemide
treatmentUsage: Heart failure volume overload, edema, especially in furosemide-resistant states; better oral bioavailability than furosemide
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits NKCC2 in the thick ascending limb of Henle's loop; 40x more potent than furosemide on a per-mg basis but equivalent when used at equivalent doses.
sideEffects: Hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hyperuricemia, metabolic alkalosis, ototoxicity, sulfa allergy cross-reactivity
contraindications: Sulfa allergy, anuria
tooltip: Loop diuretic — 40x more potent per mg than furosemide but same mechanism (NKCC2 inhibitor). Better oral bioavailability. Same side effects: hypokalemia, ototoxicity, hyperuricemia. Sulfa allergy → use ethacrynic acid.

name: Ethacrynic acid
primaryEffect: Potent diuresis; only loop diuretic safe in sulfa allergy
treatmentUsage: Heart failure, edema in patients with sulfa allergy who cannot tolerate furosemide or bumetanide
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits NKCC2 in the thick ascending limb; structurally distinct from other loop diuretics (phenoxyacetic acid derivative, not sulfonamide), making it safe in sulfa allergy.
sideEffects: Ototoxicity (more severe and potentially permanent — most ototoxic loop diuretic), hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis
contraindications: Anuria; use with caution with other ototoxic drugs (aminoglycosides)
tooltip: Only loop diuretic without sulfonamide structure — safe in sulfa allergy. Most ototoxic of loop diuretics (permanent hearing loss risk). Same diuretic mechanism as furosemide. USMLE: sulfa allergy + need for loop diuretic → ethacrynic acid.

name: Hydrochlorothiazide
primaryEffect: Moderate diuresis and vasodilation; decreases calcium excretion
treatmentUsage: Hypertension (first-line), edema, nephrolithiasis (calcium stones — reduces urinary calcium), diabetes insipidus (nephrogenic)
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits the Na⁺/Cl⁻ cotransporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule, causing modest natriuresis and diuresis. Also increases calcium reabsorption (opposite of loop diuretics).
sideEffects: Hypokalemia, hyponatremia (most likely to cause SIADH-like dilutional hyponatremia), hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia (impairs insulin release), hyperlipidemia, hypercalcemia (decreased urinary calcium), hypomagnesemia
contraindications: Sulfa allergy, severe hypokalemia, anuria, gout (relative — causes hyperuricemia)
tooltip: First-line thiazide diuretic. Inhibits NCC in DCT. Key metabolic effects: hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, hypercalcemia. Reduces calcium excretion — paradoxically used for calcium kidney stones and nephrogenic DI. Causes SIADH-like hyponatremia.

name: Chlorthalidone
primaryEffect: Longer-acting moderate diuresis and vasodilation; preferred over HCTZ for cardiovascular outcomes
treatmentUsage: Hypertension (preferred over HCTZ in many guidelines), heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, edema
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits NCC in the distal convoluted tubule; longer half-life than hydrochlorothiazide (~45-60 hours vs ~6-12 hours), leading to more sustained BP control.
sideEffects: Hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, hypercalcemia, hypomagnesemia — same as HCTZ but more prolonged duration of action
contraindications: Sulfa allergy, severe hypokalemia, anuria
tooltip: Thiazide-like diuretic with longer half-life than HCTZ. Preferred in JNC 8 and many US guidelines for primary hypertension due to superior CV outcomes data. Same metabolic side effects as HCTZ. Inhibits NCC in DCT.

name: Spironolactone
primaryEffect: Aldosterone antagonism causing mild diuresis while retaining potassium
treatmentUsage: Heart failure (HFrEF — reduces mortality), primary hyperaldosteronism, hypertension (especially resistant), cirrhotic ascites, edema, acne/hirsutism (anti-androgen), polycystic ovarian syndrome
mechanismOfAction: Competitively antagonizes aldosterone at mineralocorticoid receptors in the collecting duct, preventing Na⁺ retention and K⁺ excretion. Also blocks androgen receptors (causes anti-androgen side effects).
sideEffects: Hyperkalemia (most dangerous), gynecomastia (anti-androgen in males), menstrual irregularities in females, breast tenderness, decreased libido
contraindications: Hyperkalemia, severe renal failure (risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia), concurrent use with ACE inhibitor + ARB
tooltip: Aldosterone antagonist — blocks mineralocorticoid receptors. Reduces mortality in HFrEF. Key side effects: hyperkalemia (monitor K⁺ closely), gynecomastia in males (blocks androgen receptors). Used for primary hyperaldosteronism and ascites in cirrhosis.

name: Eplerenone
primaryEffect: Selective aldosterone antagonism with fewer anti-androgen side effects than spironolactone
treatmentUsage: Heart failure post-MI with LV dysfunction (EPHESUS trial), HFrEF, hypertension
mechanismOfAction: Selectively antagonizes mineralocorticoid (aldosterone) receptors with minimal affinity for androgen or progesterone receptors, avoiding the gynecomastia and menstrual effects of spironolactone.
sideEffects: Hyperkalemia (monitor closely), dizziness; NO gynecomastia (unlike spironolactone)
contraindications: Hyperkalemia, severe renal failure, concurrent use with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (significantly increases levels)
tooltip: Selective aldosterone antagonist — like spironolactone but without anti-androgen effects (no gynecomastia). EPHESUS trial: reduces mortality post-MI with LV dysfunction. Key risk: hyperkalemia. More expensive than spironolactone.

name: Acetazolamide
primaryEffect: Inhibits carbonic anhydrase causing bicarbonate diuresis and metabolic acidosis
treatmentUsage: Altitude sickness prevention/treatment, glaucoma (reduces aqueous humor production), metabolic alkalosis, idiopathic intracranial hypertension, epilepsy (adjunct), urine alkalinization
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits carbonic anhydrase in the proximal convoluted tubule, preventing HCO₃⁻ reabsorption; causes bicarbonate diuresis and metabolic acidosis. In the eye, reduces aqueous humor formation.
sideEffects: Metabolic acidosis (HCO₃⁻ wasting), hypokalemia, paresthesias (tingling in extremities — classic side effect), nephrolithiasis (calcium phosphate stones — alkaline urine), drowsiness, sulfa allergy cross-reactivity
contraindications: Sulfa allergy, severe hepatic disease (worsens encephalopathy by reducing NH₄⁺ excretion), metabolic acidosis
tooltip: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor. Used for altitude sickness, glaucoma, and correcting metabolic alkalosis. Classic USMLE side effects: paresthesias (tingling), metabolic acidosis (HCO₃⁻ wasting), calcium phosphate kidney stones. Sulfa cross-reactivity.

name: Quinidine
primaryEffect: Slows conduction and prolongs refractory period by blocking Na⁺ and K⁺ channels
treatmentUsage: Ventricular arrhythmias, atrial fibrillation/flutter conversion (rarely used now — largely replaced by safer agents)
mechanismOfAction: Blocks fast Na⁺ channels (intermediate kinetics) and K⁺ channels (IKr); prolongs both depolarization and repolarization, increasing action potential duration and QRS/QT intervals.
sideEffects: Cinchonism (tinnitus, headache, blurred vision, vertigo), Torsades de pointes (QT prolongation), thrombocytopenia, diarrhea, nausea, drug-induced lupus
contraindications: QT prolongation, Torsades de pointes history, complete heart block, digoxin toxicity (raises digoxin levels)
tooltip: Class IA antiarrhythmic — blocks Na⁺ (intermediate) and K⁺ channels; prolongs QT. Classic: cinchonism (tinnitus, blurred vision, headache). Causes Torsades de pointes. Rarely used today. Raises digoxin levels. Thrombocytopenia.

name: Procainamide
primaryEffect: Slows cardiac conduction; used IV for acute arrhythmia management
treatmentUsage: Ventricular arrhythmias, atrial fibrillation with WPW (preferred agent — slows accessory pathway), wide-complex tachycardia of uncertain origin
mechanismOfAction: Blocks fast Na⁺ channels (intermediate kinetics) and K⁺ channels; active metabolite (NAPA) also has K⁺ channel blocking activity. Prolongs QRS and QT.
sideEffects: Drug-induced lupus (anti-histone antibodies — most commonly associated drug; ANA positive), Torsades de pointes, agranulocytosis, hypotension with IV use
contraindications: Pre-existing QT prolongation, SLE, severe myasthenia gravis, complete AV block
tooltip: Class IA antiarrhythmic. Key USMLE: drug-induced lupus (anti-histone antibodies; more common than hydralazine lupus). Used IV for WPW with AF (slows accessory pathway). Causes Torsades. Active metabolite NAPA. Agranulocytosis risk.

name: Disopyramide
primaryEffect: Slows conduction and has potent negative inotropic and anticholinergic effects
treatmentUsage: Ventricular arrhythmias, atrial fibrillation; uniquely useful in hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (negative inotropy reduces LVOT obstruction)
mechanismOfAction: Blocks Na⁺ channels (intermediate kinetics) and K⁺ channels; has significant anticholinergic activity and is the most negatively inotropic Class I antiarrhythmic.
sideEffects: Anticholinergic effects (urinary retention, dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, confusion), negative inotropy (worsens heart failure), Torsades de pointes
contraindications: Heart failure (potent negative inotropy), glaucoma, urinary retention, QT prolongation
tooltip: Class IA antiarrhythmic. Most negatively inotropic Class I — paradoxically useful in HCM to reduce obstruction. Strong anticholinergic effects: urinary retention, dry mouth, constipation. Worsens heart failure. Causes Torsades.

name: Lidocaine
primaryEffect: Suppresses ventricular arrhythmias by preferentially blocking ischemic tissue Na⁺ channels
treatmentUsage: Ventricular tachycardia and fibrillation (IV, acute management), post-MI ventricular arrhythmias; also used as local anesthetic
mechanismOfAction: Blocks fast Na⁺ channels with fast on-off kinetics; preferentially binds depolarized (ischemic/infarcted) myocardium; shortens action potential duration — used when tissues are already depolarized.
sideEffects: CNS toxicity at high doses: perioral numbness (first sign), tinnitus, tremor, confusion, seizures; cardiovascular toxicity at very high doses
contraindications: No oral bioavailability (first-pass effect — 70%); severe hepatic disease (reduced metabolism); pre-existing AV block
tooltip: Class IB antiarrhythmic — fast kinetics. IV only (massive first-pass effect). Preferentially blocks ischemic/depolarized myocardium. CNS toxicity progression: perioral numbness → tinnitus → tremor → seizures. Used for ventricular arrhythmias post-MI.

name: Mexiletine
primaryEffect: Oral analog of lidocaine for chronic ventricular arrhythmia suppression
treatmentUsage: Chronic ventricular arrhythmias, long QT syndrome (type 3 — SCN5A mutation), myotonic dystrophy
mechanismOfAction: Oral Class IB Na⁺ channel blocker with fast kinetics; same mechanism as lidocaine but with oral bioavailability; shortens action potential duration.
sideEffects: GI side effects (nausea, vomiting — take with food), tremor, dizziness, nystagmus, CNS effects similar to lidocaine but less severe
contraindications: Significant AV block, cardiogenic shock
tooltip: Oral lidocaine analog — Class IB. Used for chronic ventricular arrhythmias when IV lidocaine is needed long-term. GI side effects (take with food). Useful in LQT type 3 (SCN5A) — shortens action potential. Tremor and dizziness.

name: Flecainide
primaryEffect: Potent Na⁺ channel blockade significantly slowing conduction velocity
treatmentUsage: Atrial fibrillation/flutter (paroxysmal, in structurally normal hearts), SVT; "pill in the pocket" for AF cardioversion
mechanismOfAction: Blocks fast Na⁺ channels with slow kinetics (slow on/off), markedly slowing conduction velocity and widening QRS; minimal effect on repolarization.
sideEffects: Proarrhythmic (can convert AF to flutter with 1:1 conduction — must give AV nodal blocker first), ventricular arrhythmias; dizziness, visual disturbances
contraindications: Structural heart disease, prior MI, LV dysfunction, HFrEF (CAST trial — increased mortality post-MI), AV block
tooltip: Class IC — most potent Na⁺ channel blocker. CAST trial: increased mortality in post-MI patients with PVCs — contraindicated in structural heart disease. Used only in structurally normal hearts for AF. Must pretreat with AV nodal blocker (can cause 1:1 AF→flutter).

name: Propafenone
primaryEffect: Potent Na⁺ channel blockade with mild β-blocking activity
treatmentUsage: Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation/flutter (structurally normal heart), SVT
mechanismOfAction: Blocks fast Na⁺ channels (slow kinetics) and has mild β-adrenergic receptor blocking activity; slows conduction velocity and slightly reduces heart rate.
sideEffects: Proarrhythmic (same risks as flecainide), bronchospasm (from β-blocking activity), bitter taste, GI upset, dizziness
contraindications: Structural heart disease, post-MI, HFrEF, asthma/COPD (β-blocking activity), AV block
tooltip: Class IC antiarrhythmic with mild β-blocking effect. Same contraindications as flecainide: avoid in structural heart disease (proarrhythmic). Bronchospasm risk (β2 block). Structurally normal AF only. Bitter taste is a distinguishing side effect.

name: Amiodarone
primaryEffect: Prolongs action potential and refractory period; complex multi-channel blocker
treatmentUsage: Ventricular fibrillation/tachycardia (ACLS — first-line for shock-refractory VF), atrial fibrillation (rate and rhythm control), WPW, refractory arrhythmias
mechanismOfAction: Blocks K⁺ channels (primary mechanism — prolongs repolarization), Na⁺ channels, Ca²⁺ channels, and β-adrenergic receptors; class I, II, III, and IV activity. Contains 37% iodine by weight.
sideEffects: Pulmonary toxicity (most serious — interstitial pneumonitis), thyroid dysfunction (hypo or hyperthyroidism — iodine-rich), hepatotoxicity (elevated LFTs), corneal microdeposits (nearly universal, usually asymptomatic), blue-gray skin photosensitivity, peripheral neuropathy; very long half-life (~40-55 days)
contraindications: Severe sinus node dysfunction, AV block without pacemaker, iodine allergy (relative), thyroid disease (relative), pregnancy
tooltip: Class III antiarrhythmic with class I/II/IV activity too. Most toxic major antiarrhythmic — monitor PFTs, TFTs, LFTs, eyes. Pulmonary toxicity is most serious. Contains iodine → thyroid disease. Blue-gray skin. Half-life ~50 days. First-line in ACLS for VF/VT.

name: Sotalol
primaryEffect: Prolongs repolarization (K⁺ block) with β-blocking activity
treatmentUsage: Ventricular arrhythmias, atrial fibrillation/flutter (rhythm control), maintenance of sinus rhythm
mechanismOfAction: Blocks K⁺ channels (IKr) prolonging the action potential and refractory period (Class III), plus non-selective β-adrenergic receptor blockade (Class II); both effects are proarrhythmic.
sideEffects: Torsades de pointes (significant QT prolongation — most important concern), bradycardia, fatigue, bronchospasm (β-blocking), dizziness
contraindications: QTc >500ms, Torsades history, creatinine clearance <40 mL/min (renally cleared — dose reduction required), asthma
tooltip: Class III (K⁺ block) + Class II (β-block) antiarrhythmic. Major risk: Torsades de pointes from QT prolongation. Renally cleared — reduce dose in CKD. Initiation requires inpatient telemetry monitoring (QT monitoring). Bronchospasm from β2 block.

name: Dofetilide
primaryEffect: Pure K⁺ channel (IKr) blockade prolonging repolarization
treatmentUsage: Maintenance of sinus rhythm in atrial fibrillation/flutter; conversion of AF to sinus rhythm
mechanismOfAction: Selectively blocks the rapid delayed rectifier K⁺ current (IKr), prolonging action potential duration and refractoriness without Na⁺ or β-blocking effects.
sideEffects: Torsades de pointes (QT prolongation — highest concern; QTc must be <440ms before initiation), headache, dizziness
contraindications: QTc >440ms (>500ms with bundle branch block), CrCl <20 mL/min, concurrent use with QT-prolonging drugs, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia
tooltip: Pure Class III K⁺ channel blocker (IKr). Requires in-hospital initiation with continuous QT monitoring for 3 days (FDA requirement). Dose must be strictly adjusted for renal function. Major risk: Torsades. Used specifically for AF/flutter rhythm control.

name: Adenosine
primaryEffect: Transiently blocks AV node conduction to terminate re-entrant SVTs
treatmentUsage: PSVT/SVT termination (first-line), diagnosis of wide-complex tachycardia (if SVT — terminates; if VT — may not affect), pharmacologic cardiac stress testing
mechanismOfAction: Activates A1 adenosine receptors in the AV node, hyperpolarizing nodal cells via K⁺ channel opening and inhibiting Ca²⁺ currents, causing transient complete AV block.
sideEffects: Transient asystole (complete heart block — intentional and brief), flushing, chest tightness, severe dyspnea (very common — warn patient), anxiety, bronchospasm
contraindications: Pre-existing AV block or sick sinus syndrome (without pacemaker), asthma (bronchospasm), WPW (can precipitate VF via accessory pathway), use with dipyridamole (potentiates effect — reduce dose); theophylline blocks its effect
tooltip: Half-life ~10 seconds. First-line for SVT — causes transient AV block to break re-entry. Warn patient about chest tightness and flushing (very unpleasant but brief). Blocked by caffeine/theophylline (methylxanthines). Potentiated by dipyridamole. Avoid in asthma and WPW.

name: Digoxin
primaryEffect: Positive inotropy with negative chronotropy; narrow therapeutic index
treatmentUsage: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (symptom improvement, not mortality), atrial fibrillation rate control (especially with HF)
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, increasing intracellular Na⁺, which reduces Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger activity, elevating intracellular Ca²⁺ and contractility. Increases vagal tone (negative chronotropy via AV node slowing).
sideEffects: Narrow therapeutic index — toxicity includes: nausea/vomiting, anorexia, yellow-green visual halos (xanthopsia), bradycardia, heart block, any arrhythmia; toxicity worsened by hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypercalcemia, hypothyroidism, and quinidine
contraindications: Ventricular fibrillation, pre-excitation syndromes (WPW — can precipitate VF), hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy
tooltip: Cardiac glycoside — inhibits Na/K-ATPase → positive inotropy, negative chronotropy. Classic toxicity: yellow-green halos (xanthopsia), nausea, any arrhythmia. Hypokalemia worsens toxicity. Treat toxicity with digoxin Fab antibody fragments. Very narrow therapeutic index.

name: Ivabradine
primaryEffect: Reduces heart rate by blocking the funny current (If) in the SA node
treatmentUsage: Stable heart failure (HFrEF with HR ≥70 bpm on maximally tolerated beta-blockers), stable angina (when beta-blockers contraindicated)
mechanismOfAction: Selectively blocks the hyperpolarization-activated cyclic nucleotide-gated (HCN) If (funny) current in the SA node, slowing spontaneous depolarization and reducing heart rate without affecting contractility or conduction.
sideEffects: Bradycardia, phosphenes (brief luminous visual disturbances — patient sees halos or flickering lights, classic side effect), atrial fibrillation (increased risk), headache
contraindications: Resting HR <60 bpm, sick sinus syndrome, severe hepatic impairment, concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors, atrial fibrillation (not effective — requires sinus rhythm)
tooltip: Blocks SA node If (funny) channel — reduces HR without affecting contractility. Used in HFrEF when beta-blockers inadequate or contraindicated. Classic side effect: phosphenes (luminous visual disturbances). Requires sinus rhythm to work. Increases AF risk.

name: Nitroglycerin
primaryEffect: Venodilation (primarily) and arterial dilation reducing preload, afterload, and myocardial O₂ demand
treatmentUsage: Acute angina relief (sublingual — first-line), hypertensive emergency, acute decompensated heart failure (IV), acute MI (IV), esophageal spasm
mechanismOfAction: Enzymatically converted to nitric oxide (NO), which activates guanylyl cyclase → increased cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation. Primarily venodilates at low doses, also arterially dilates at higher doses.
sideEffects: Headache (most common — vasodilation of meningeal vessels), hypotension, reflex tachycardia, tolerance with continuous use (requires nitrate-free interval), methemoglobinemia (rare, high doses)
contraindications: Concurrent PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil — severe hypotension), severe aortic stenosis (preload dependent), hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, right ventricular infarction
tooltip: Nitric oxide donor — venodilates primarily, arterially dilates at higher doses. Sublingual for acute angina attack. Avoid with PDE5 inhibitors (life-threatening hypotension). Classic side effect: headache. Tolerance develops with continuous use — need nitrate-free interval.

name: Isosorbide mononitrate
primaryEffect: Long-acting venodilation and arterial dilation for chronic angina prophylaxis
treatmentUsage: Chronic stable angina prophylaxis, heart failure (especially combined with hydralazine in HFrEF — V-HeFT trial)
mechanismOfAction: Generates nitric oxide → cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation; active compound (not a prodrug like isosorbide dinitrate), providing more predictable drug levels.
sideEffects: Headache, hypotension, dizziness, tolerance (nitrate-free interval required — usually 8-10 hours overnight)
contraindications: Concurrent PDE5 inhibitors, severe aortic stenosis, hypotension, right ventricular infarction
tooltip: Long-acting oral nitrate for chronic angina prophylaxis. Active compound (not prodrug). Nitrate tolerance requires daily nitrate-free interval (~8-10 hours, typically overnight). Combined with hydralazine in HFrEF patients intolerant of ACE inhibitors/ARBs (V-HeFT trial).

name: Atorvastatin
primaryEffect: Reduces LDL cholesterol by inhibiting hepatic cholesterol synthesis
treatmentUsage: Hypercholesterolemia, cardiovascular risk reduction (primary and secondary prevention), atherosclerosis, post-ACS (high-intensity statin), stroke prevention
mechanismOfAction: Competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme of cholesterol synthesis; reduces hepatic cholesterol → upregulates LDL receptors → increased LDL clearance from blood.
sideEffects: Myopathy (muscle pain/weakness — monitor CK), rhabdomyolysis (rare but serious — myoglobinuria → acute kidney injury), hepatotoxicity (transaminase elevation — monitor LFTs), hyperglycemia (small increased diabetes risk)
contraindications: Active liver disease, pregnancy (teratogenic), concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (raises statin levels → myopathy risk)
tooltip: High-intensity statin (among most potent LDL reducers). Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase. Key USMLE side effects: myopathy/rhabdomyolysis (monitor CK if muscle pain), hepatotoxicity. Contraindicated in pregnancy. CYP3A4 interactions. First-line for secondary CV prevention.

name: Rosuvastatin
primaryEffect: Most potent statin for LDL reduction; also raises HDL significantly
treatmentUsage: Hypercholesterolemia (highest potency — preferred for very high LDL), cardiovascular risk reduction, atherosclerosis
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase; most potent statin (can reduce LDL by 50-60%); renally excreted (unlike most statins that are hepatically metabolized — fewer CYP450 interactions).
sideEffects: Myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, hepatotoxicity, proteinuria (high doses), dipstick-positive hematuria
contraindications: Active liver disease, pregnancy, severe renal impairment (renally excreted — accumulates)
tooltip: Highest-potency statin — greatest LDL reduction. Renally (not hepatically) excreted — fewer CYP drug interactions. Dose reduce in severe CKD. Same class risks: myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, hepatotoxicity, teratogen. Raises HDL more than most statins.

name: Simvastatin
primaryEffect: Moderate LDL reduction; significant CYP3A4 drug interactions
treatmentUsage: Hypercholesterolemia, cardiovascular risk reduction (4S trial — landmark mortality benefit)
mechanismOfAction: Prodrug converted to active hydroxy acid form; inhibits HMG-CoA reductase; extensively metabolized by CYP3A4, creating multiple clinically significant drug interactions.
sideEffects: Myopathy (significant — especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors like amiodarone, diltiazem, verapamil, azole antifungals, macrolides), rhabdomyolysis, hepatotoxicity
contraindications: Active liver disease, pregnancy, concurrent amiodarone >20mg dose, concurrent strong CYP3A4 inhibitors, concurrent cyclosporine or gemfibrozil (markedly increases myopathy risk)
tooltip: Moderate-intensity statin. Major CYP3A4 interactions — amiodarone, diltiazem, verapamil, azoles, macrolides all raise simvastatin levels → rhabdomyolysis. Never use >20mg with amiodarone. Avoid with gemfibrozil. 4S trial showed mortality benefit post-MI.

name: Ezetimibe
primaryEffect: Reduces cholesterol absorption in the small intestine
treatmentUsage: Hypercholesterolemia (as add-on to statin therapy when LDL goal not met), primary hypercholesterolemia
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits the NPC1L1 (Niemann-Pick C1-like 1) transporter in intestinal brush border cells, blocking absorption of dietary and biliary cholesterol; reduces LDL by ~20%.
sideEffects: GI discomfort, diarrhea, myopathy (rare, especially when combined with statin), hepatitis (rare)
contraindications: Active liver disease (when combined with statins), moderate to severe hepatic impairment
tooltip: Inhibits intestinal NPC1L1 cholesterol transporter — reduces dietary/biliary cholesterol absorption. Used as add-on to statins for additional LDL lowering (~20% reduction). IMPROVE-IT trial: adding ezetimibe to statin reduced CV events post-ACS. Well-tolerated.

name: Niacin
primaryEffect: Raises HDL most effectively of any drug; also lowers triglycerides and LDL
treatmentUsage: Dyslipidemia (raising HDL, lowering triglycerides), pellagra (nicotinic acid deficiency), combined with statins for mixed dyslipidemia
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits lipolysis in adipose tissue (reduces FFA flux to liver → less VLDL synthesis), decreases hepatic VLDL production, and reduces hepatic clearance of HDL; mechanism of HDL elevation involves HCA2 receptor activation.
sideEffects: Flushing (most common and bothersome — prostaglandin-mediated; reduced by taking aspirin 30 min before or using extended-release formulation), hyperglycemia, hyperuricemia (gout), hepatotoxicity (especially extended-release), nausea
contraindications: Active liver disease, peptic ulcer disease, gout (worsens), diabetes (worsens glycemic control)
tooltip: Raises HDL more than any other drug. Most common side effect: flushing (prostaglandin-mediated — prevent with aspirin 30 min before dose). Hyperglycemia and hyperuricemia are important metabolic effects. Extended-release form has more hepatotoxicity risk. AIM-HIGH trial failed to show CV benefit over statin alone.

name: Aspirin
primaryEffect: Irreversibly inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase, preventing thromboxane A2-mediated platelet aggregation
treatmentUsage: Acute MI (first-line — immediate 325mg), secondary prevention of MI and stroke, unstable angina, post-PCI, atrial fibrillation stroke prevention (lower efficacy than anticoagulants), fever, pain, anti-inflammatory
mechanismOfAction: Irreversibly acetylates and inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) and COX-2. In platelets (which lack nuclei): prevents thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis for platelet lifetime (~7-10 days). At low doses, primarily COX-1/platelet; higher doses also anti-inflammatory.
sideEffects: GI bleeding, peptic ulcer, dyspepsia, tinnitus (salicylism — high doses), bronchospasm in aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD/Samter's triad), Reye syndrome in children with viral illness
contraindications: Children with viral illness (Reye syndrome), aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD), active GI bleeding, allergy
tooltip: Irreversibly inhibits COX-1 in platelets → prevents TXA2-mediated aggregation. Given immediately in STEMI/ACS. Platelet effect lasts 7-10 days (platelet lifespan). Classic: Reye syndrome in children with viral illness. AERD/Samter's triad: aspirin + asthma + nasal polyps.

name: Clopidogrel
primaryEffect: Irreversibly inhibits ADP-mediated platelet activation via P2Y12 receptor blockade
treatmentUsage: ACS (dual antiplatelet with aspirin — DAPT), post-PCI with stent, peripheral artery disease, secondary stroke prevention (alternative to aspirin), aspirin allergy
mechanismOfAction: Prodrug requiring CYP2C19 activation to active thiolactone metabolite; irreversibly blocks P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets, preventing ADP-mediated platelet aggregation and glycoprotein IIb/IIIa activation.
sideEffects: Bleeding (most important), TTP (thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura — rare but serious), rash, GI discomfort; reduced efficacy in CYP2C19 poor metabolizers (genetic testing important)
contraindications: Active bleeding, CYP2C19 poor metabolizer status (consider ticagrelor or prasugrel instead), concurrent PPI use (omeprazole — reduces activation)
tooltip: Thienopyridine prodrug — requires CYP2C19 activation. Irreversibly blocks P2Y12 ADP receptor. CYP2C19 poor metabolizers have reduced efficacy — switch to ticagrelor. Omeprazole reduces its activation (use pantoprazole instead). TTP is rare but serious. Standard DAPT with aspirin post-ACS.

name: Ticagrelor
primaryEffect: Reversible P2Y12 ADP receptor blockade; not a prodrug — faster onset than clopidogrel
treatmentUsage: ACS (preferred over clopidogrel in PLATO trial — reduced mortality), post-PCI, STEMI (with aspirin as DAPT)
mechanismOfAction: Directly and reversibly inhibits P2Y12 ADP receptor (not a prodrug — no activation required); also inhibits adenosine reuptake (contributes to dyspnea side effect).
sideEffects: Bleeding (more than clopidogrel), dyspnea (non-bronchospastic — from adenosine reuptake inhibition; unique side effect), bradycardia, ventricular pauses; reversible (stopped 5 days before surgery vs 5-7 days for clopidogrel)
contraindications: History of intracranial hemorrhage, active pathological bleeding, severe hepatic impairment, concurrent strong CYP3A4 inhibitors
tooltip: P2Y12 blocker — reversible (unlike clopidogrel) and not a prodrug (no CYP2C19 needed). PLATO trial: superior to clopidogrel in ACS mortality reduction. Unique side effect: dyspnea (adenosine reuptake inhibition) — common but not bronchospasm. Hold 5 days before surgery.

name: Heparin
primaryEffect: Rapidly anticoagulates by potentiating antithrombin III activity
treatmentUsage: Acute VTE treatment, DVT/PE, ACS (STEMI, NSTEMI), PCI, bridging anticoagulation, hemodialysis circuit anticoagulation
mechanismOfAction: Binds antithrombin III (AT-III), causing conformational change that dramatically accelerates AT-III inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa; UFH also inhibits thrombin directly via AT-III bridging.
sideEffects: Bleeding, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT type I — benign, early, mild platelet decrease; HIT type II — immune-mediated, serious, IgG antibody to PF4-heparin complex, paradoxical thrombosis), osteoporosis (long-term use), elevated LFTs
contraindications: Active bleeding (except life-threatening indications), severe thrombocytopenia, history of HIT type II
tooltip: Potentiates antithrombin III. Monitored by aPTT. HIT type II is the critical complication — immune IgG to PF4-heparin complex → paradoxical thrombosis despite low platelets. Treatment: stop heparin immediately, use argatroban or bivalirudin. Reversal: protamine sulfate.

name: Warfarin
primaryEffect: Oral anticoagulation via vitamin K-dependent clotting factor inhibition
treatmentUsage: Atrial fibrillation stroke prevention, VTE treatment and prevention, mechanical heart valve anticoagulation, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism
mechanismOfAction: Inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKOR), preventing regeneration of active vitamin K; blocks carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X (2, 7, 9, 10) and proteins C and S. Monitored by INR (PT).
sideEffects: Bleeding (most common), skin necrosis (early — from protein C/S deficiency before full anticoagulation; give heparin bridge), warfarin embryopathy/teratogenicity (nasal hypoplasia, bone defects), multiple drug and food interactions
contraindications: Pregnancy (Category X — embryopathy), active bleeding, inadequate INR monitoring access; many drug interactions requiring dose adjustment
tooltip: Vitamin K antagonist — inhibits VKOR, blocks factors II/VII/IX/X and proteins C/S. Monitored by INR (target 2-3, or 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valves). Multiple drug/food interactions. Skin necrosis early on (protein C drops first — bridge with heparin). Reversal: vitamin K, FFP, 4-factor PCC.

name: Apixaban
primaryEffect: Direct oral anticoagulation by factor Xa inhibition
treatmentUsage: Atrial fibrillation stroke prevention (ARISTOTLE trial — superior to warfarin), VTE treatment and prevention, post-orthopedic surgery thromboprophylaxis
mechanismOfAction: Directly and reversibly inhibits factor Xa, blocking the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin without requiring antithrombin; does not require routine monitoring.
sideEffects: Bleeding (including intracranial and GI hemorrhage — lower rates than warfarin), nausea
contraindications: Active major bleeding, severe hepatic impairment, pregnancy, mechanical heart valves (use warfarin instead), severe renal impairment (CrCl <15 mL/min)
tooltip: Direct factor Xa inhibitor — DOAC. ARISTOTLE trial: superior to warfarin in AF (less stroke, less bleeding, less mortality). No routine monitoring needed. Reversal: andexanet alfa. Twice daily dosing. Do NOT use with mechanical heart valves.

name: Rivaroxaban
primaryEffect: Direct oral anticoagulation via factor Xa inhibition; convenient once-daily dosing
treatmentUsage: Atrial fibrillation stroke prevention, VTE treatment and prevention, post-orthopedic surgery thromboprophylaxis, reducing CV events in stable CAD/PAD (low dose)
mechanismOfAction: Directly inhibits factor Xa; once-daily dosing (taken with evening meal to improve absorption); partially renally cleared.
sideEffects: Bleeding (GI bleeding more than apixaban), nausea; higher GI bleed risk among DOACs
contraindications: Active major bleeding, severe hepatic impairment, pregnancy, mechanical heart valves, severe renal impairment
tooltip: Direct factor Xa inhibitor DOAC. Once-daily (take with evening meal for absorption). Higher GI bleeding risk than apixaban. Reversal: andexanet alfa. ROCKET-AF: non-inferior to warfarin in AF. Partially renal — reduce dose in CKD. Avoid with mechanical valves.

name: Dabigatran
primaryEffect: Direct oral thrombin inhibition (factor IIa)
treatmentUsage: Atrial fibrillation stroke prevention (RE-LY trial — superior warfarin alternative), VTE treatment and prevention
mechanismOfAction: Directly and reversibly inhibits thrombin (factor IIa), preventing fibrinogen conversion to fibrin and platelet activation. Prodrug (dabigatran etexilate) activated by esterases.
sideEffects: Bleeding (GI bleeding highest among DOACs, especially at higher dose), dyspepsia (most common non-bleeding side effect — take with food or dose reduction), nausea
contraindications: Mechanical heart valves, severe renal impairment (CrCl <15-30 mL/min — renally excreted), pregnancy, active bleeding
tooltip: Direct thrombin inhibitor — only DOAC with specific reversal agent: idarucizumab (Praxbind). Dyspepsia very common — patient education key. RE-LY trial: superior to warfarin at higher dose for stroke prevention. Renally cleared — contraindicated in severe CKD. No use with mechanical valves.

name: Alteplase
primaryEffect: Dissolves thrombi by converting plasminogen to plasmin
treatmentUsage: STEMI (if PCI unavailable, door-to-balloon >120 min), massive PE with hemodynamic compromise, acute ischemic stroke (within 3-4.5 hours of onset), acute limb ischemia
mechanismOfAction: Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) — binds fibrin in thrombus and converts plasminogen to plasmin, which cleaves fibrin and dissolves clots. Relatively fibrin-selective compared to streptokinase.
sideEffects: Intracranial hemorrhage (most feared complication — 1-2% in stroke), systemic bleeding, angioedema (ACE inhibitor-like — bradykinin), reperfusion arrhythmias (after STEMI)
contraindications: Prior intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke within 3 months (relative), active internal bleeding, severe uncontrolled hypertension, recent CNS surgery, intracranial neoplasm
tooltip: tPA — fibrin-selective thrombolytic. Used in STEMI (if PCI unavailable), massive PE, and ischemic stroke (within 3-4.5 hrs). Biggest risk: intracranial hemorrhage. Fibrin-selective (better than streptokinase). Monitor for reperfusion arrhythmias post-STEMI.

name: Hydralazine
primaryEffect: Arteriolar vasodilation reducing afterload; combined with nitrates for HFrEF
treatmentUsage: Hypertension (especially in pregnancy — safe), hypertensive emergency in pregnancy (preeclampsia), heart failure (combined with isosorbide dinitrate — V-HeFT trial, especially in African American patients), heart failure when ACE inhibitors/ARBs not tolerated
mechanismOfAction: Directly relaxes arteriolar smooth muscle by unclear mechanisms (possibly K⁺ channel opening or reduced IP3); reduces SVR and afterload without affecting venous capacitance — causes reflex tachycardia.
sideEffects: Reflex tachycardia (most common — use with beta-blocker or nitrate), fluid retention, headache, lupus-like syndrome (anti-histone antibodies — especially with high doses, slow acetylators), angina precipitation (from tachycardia)
contraindications: Aortic aneurysm, ischemic heart disease (relative — tachycardia increases O₂ demand), mitral valve rheumatic disease
tooltip: Arteriolar vasodilator — reduces afterload. Classic USMLE: drug-induced lupus (anti-histone antibodies; high doses, slow acetylators). Safe in pregnancy. Causes reflex tachycardia — combine with β-blocker. V-HeFT: hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate reduces mortality in HFrEF (especially Black patients).

name: Minoxidil
primaryEffect: Potent arteriolar vasodilation via K⁺ channel opening
treatmentUsage: Severe refractory hypertension (oral), androgenetic alopecia (topical)
mechanismOfAction: Opens ATP-sensitive K⁺ channels in vascular smooth muscle, causing hyperpolarization and arteriolar vasodilation; sulfated metabolite (minoxidil sulfate) is the active form.
sideEffects: Hypertrichosis (excess hair growth — universal; cosmetically problematic), fluid retention (can be severe — combine with loop diuretic), reflex tachycardia, pericardial effusion (long-term use)
contraindications: Pheochromocytoma (reflex catecholamine surge), pericardial effusion
tooltip: Potent arteriolar vasodilator (K⁺ channel opener). Reserved for severe refractory hypertension. Classic side effect: hypertrichosis (universal). Fluid retention requires concurrent loop diuretic. Pericardial effusion with long-term use. Topical form used for hair loss (alopecia).

name: Sodium nitroprusside
primaryEffect: Rapid, titratable reduction of both preload and afterload via NO release
treatmentUsage: Hypertensive emergency (most powerful IV agent), acute decompensated heart failure (afterload reduction), aortic dissection (combined with beta-blocker), controlled hypotension in surgery
mechanismOfAction: Spontaneously releases nitric oxide → activates guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation of both arteries and veins (balanced vasodilation — unlike nitroglycerin which is primarily venous).
sideEffects: Cyanide toxicity (most serious — from CN⁻ release; metabolized to thiocyanate, which accumulates in renal failure); thiocyanate toxicity; hypotension, reflex tachycardia; coronary steal (worsens ischemia by dilating non-ischemic vessels)
contraindications: Compensatory hypertension (e.g., AV shunt), hepatic disease (impairs cyanide detoxification); use with extreme caution in renal failure
tooltip: Most potent IV antihypertensive — balanced arterial and venous vasodilation via NO. ICU use only. Cyanide toxicity is the critical concern (CN⁻ released → accumulates with prolonged use or renal/hepatic failure). Monitor thiocyanate levels. Coronary steal effect.

name: Fenoldopam
primaryEffect: Dopamine D1 receptor-mediated vasodilation with preserved renal perfusion
treatmentUsage: Hypertensive emergency (IV), especially when renal protection is desired (e.g., contrast-induced nephropathy prophylaxis, CKD patients)
mechanismOfAction: Selectively activates dopamine D1 receptors in peripheral arteries and the kidney, causing vasodilation and increased renal blood flow/sodium excretion; does not cross the blood-brain barrier.
sideEffects: Reflex tachycardia, headache, flushing, hypokalemia (increases natriuresis), dose-related hypotension
contraindications: Glaucoma (increases intraocular pressure), sulfite allergy (contains sulfite preservative)
tooltip: Selective D1 receptor agonist — peripheral vasodilation plus increased renal blood flow. Preferred in hypertensive emergency when renal protection is a priority. Does not cross BBB (no CNS effects). Key side effects: reflex tachycardia, hypokalemia. Avoid in glaucoma.

name: Sacubitril/valsartan
primaryEffect: Combined neprilysin inhibition and AT1 receptor blockade reducing cardiac remodeling
treatmentUsage: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) — PARADIGM-HF trial showed superiority over enalapril in reducing CV death and HF hospitalization
mechanismOfAction: Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin (neutral endopeptidase) → prevents degradation of natriuretic peptides (BNP, ANP), bradykinin, and substance P → vasodilation, natriuresis, reduced cardiac remodeling. Valsartan blocks AT1 receptor. Combined effect reduces preload, afterload, and fibrosis.
sideEffects: Hypotension (common — start at low dose), hyperkalemia, angioedema (increased risk because neprilysin inhibition raises bradykinin — do NOT combine with ACE inhibitor), renal impairment
contraindications: Concurrent ACE inhibitor use (36-hour washout required before starting — angioedema risk), prior history of angioedema, pregnancy, concurrent aliskiren in diabetics
tooltip: ARNI (PARADIGM-HF trial) — superior to ACE inhibitor alone in HFrEF. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin → ↑ natriuretic peptides. Valsartan blocks AT1. Key warning: NEVER combine with ACE inhibitor (angioedema). Require 36-hr washout from ACEi. Check for hypotension and hyperkalemia.

name: Dobutamine
primaryEffect: Positive inotropy via β1 stimulation without significant increase in heart rate at low doses
treatmentUsage: Acute decompensated heart failure (short-term hemodynamic support), cardiogenic shock, cardiac stress testing (dobutamine stress echo)
mechanismOfAction: Synthetic catecholamine that primarily stimulates β1-adrenergic receptors, increasing myocardial contractility and cardiac output; also stimulates β2 (vasodilation) and mild α1 (vasoconstriction), resulting in little net vascular resistance change.
sideEffects: Tachycardia, arrhythmias (atrial and ventricular), increased myocardial oxygen demand, hypertension, hypotension (from β2 vasodilation at low doses), tolerance with prolonged use
contraindications: Obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM), idiopathic hypertrophic subaortic stenosis (IHSS), uncorrected hypovolemia
tooltip: Synthetic catecholamine — β1 agonist (positive inotrope). Used for acute decompensated HF and cardiogenic shock. Also used in dobutamine stress echocardiography. Key risks: tachycardia and arrhythmias. Does not raise BP like norepinephrine — preferred when vasodilation is acceptable.

name: Norepinephrine
primaryEffect: Potent vasoconstriction and positive inotropy; first-line vasopressor for septic shock
treatmentUsage: Septic shock (first-line vasopressor — Surviving Sepsis Campaign), distributive shock, cardiogenic shock with hypotension
mechanismOfAction: Potent α1-adrenergic agonist (vasoconstriction) with β1 activity (positive inotropy and chronotropy); little β2 activity. Increases SVR and MAP; reflex bradycardia can occur from baroreflex.
sideEffects: Peripheral ischemia/necrosis (extravasation — give through central line), reflex bradycardia, hypertension, decreased renal/mesenteric perfusion
contraindications: Hypovolemia (must correct volume first), extravasation risk in peripheral IV
tooltip: First-line vasopressor for septic shock. Primarily α1 (vasoconstriction) + β1 (inotropy). Must be given via central line (causes necrosis if extravasates). Reflex bradycardia from raised BP. More pressor effect than dopamine. Surpassed dopamine as first-line in sepsis guidelines.

name: Epinephrine
primaryEffect: Potent α and β adrenergic stimulation; first-line for anaphylaxis and cardiac arrest
treatmentUsage: Anaphylaxis (IM, first-line), cardiac arrest (ACLS — VF, pulseless VT, asystole, PEA), severe asthma, croup, septic shock (adjunct vasopressor), local anesthetic with vasoconstrictor
mechanismOfAction: Activates α1 (vasoconstriction), β1 (positive inotropy and chronotropy), and β2 (bronchodilation, vasodilation, decreased mast cell mediator release) adrenergic receptors. In anaphylaxis, reverses bronchoconstriction and prevents systemic vasodilation.
sideEffects: Tachycardia, hypertension, arrhythmias, increased myocardial O₂ demand, anxiety, tremor, tissue necrosis if extravasates
contraindications: No absolute contraindications in anaphylaxis (benefit always outweighs risk); cocaine toxicity (relative — α-adrenergic effects can worsen hypertension)
tooltip: Endogenous catecholamine — α1+β1+β2 agonist. FIRST-LINE for anaphylaxis (IM thigh). ACLS for cardiac arrest. β2 reverses bronchoconstriction; α1 reverses vasodilation in anaphylaxis. No absolute contraindications in anaphylaxis. Most essential emergency drug.

name: Atropine
primaryEffect: Blocks muscarinic receptors, increasing heart rate and reducing secretions
treatmentUsage: Symptomatic bradycardia (ACLS — first-line), organophosphate/nerve agent poisoning, preoperative drying of secretions, reversal of neuromuscular blockade (with neostigmine), dilating eye for exam
mechanismOfAction: Competitive antagonist of muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (M1, M2, M3); at the heart (M2), blocks vagal slowing and increases SA node firing rate and AV node conduction.
sideEffects: Tachycardia, dry mouth (most common), urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision (cycloplegia), confusion/hallucinations (especially in elderly), hyperthermia, flushing — mnemonic: 'dry as a bone, blind as a bat, red as a beet, hot as a hare, mad as a hatter'
contraindications: Narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention/BPH, ileus, myasthenia gravis, tachycardia
tooltip: Muscarinic antagonist — blocks vagal tone, increases HR. First-line for symptomatic bradycardia (ACLS). Antidote for organophosphate poisoning. Classic anticholinergic toxidrome: dry/hot/flushed skin, tachycardia, urinary retention, confusion ('dry as bone, blind as bat, red as beet').

name: Evolocumab
primaryEffect: Dramatically lowers LDL by preventing PCSK9-mediated degradation of LDL receptors
treatmentUsage: Familial hypercholesterolemia, very high cardiovascular risk when statins inadequate, statin intolerance, very high LDL (can lower LDL by 60-70%)
mechanismOfAction: Monoclonal antibody that binds and inhibits PCSK9 (proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9); normally PCSK9 degrades LDL receptors in the liver — inhibition leads to more LDL receptors on hepatocytes → increased LDL clearance.
sideEffects: Injection site reactions, nasopharyngitis, back pain, influenza-like symptoms; neurocognitive effects (rare — post-marketing reports)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity; expensive — often requires prior authorization
tooltip: Monoclonal antibody inhibiting PCSK9 — prevents LDL receptor degradation → dramatic LDL reduction (60-70%). FOURIER trial: reduced CV events in high-risk patients on maximally tolerated statins. Subcutaneous injection every 2-4 weeks. Expensive. Used for familial hypercholesterolemia and statin intolerance.

name: Gemfibrozil
primaryEffect: Reduces triglycerides and raises HDL via PPAR-α activation
treatmentUsage: Hypertriglyceridemia, mixed dyslipidemia (raising HDL, lowering TG), pancreatitis prevention from severe hypertriglyceridemia
mechanismOfAction: Activates PPAR-α (peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha) in hepatocytes → increased lipoprotein lipase expression → increased TG clearance, decreased VLDL production, increased HDL synthesis.
sideEffects: Myopathy (greatly increased risk when combined with statins — CYP2C8 interaction increases statin levels), cholelithiasis (gallstones — increases biliary cholesterol secretion), GI upset, hepatotoxicity
contraindications: Concurrent statin use (especially simvastatin or lovastatin — high myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk), hepatic or severe renal disease, gallbladder disease
tooltip: PPAR-α agonist — lowers triglycerides, raises HDL. Major risk: myopathy/rhabdomyolysis when combined with statins (CYP2C8 raises statin levels — gemfibrozil is particularly dangerous with statins vs fenofibrate). Gallstones risk. Use fenofibrate instead if statin combination needed.

name: Prasugrel
primaryEffect: More potent and faster irreversible P2Y12 blockade than clopidogrel
treatmentUsage: ACS managed with PCI (preferred in TRITON-TIMI trial over clopidogrel for PCI patients), STEMI
mechanismOfAction: Thienopyridine prodrug rapidly converted (faster and more complete than clopidogrel) to active metabolite; irreversibly blocks P2Y12 ADP receptor; not dependent on CYP2C19 activity.
sideEffects: Bleeding (significantly more than clopidogrel — especially intracranial and fatal bleeding), hypersensitivity
contraindications: Prior stroke or TIA (absolute — net harm in TRITON-TIMI), age >75 years (relative — increased bleeding, minimal benefit), body weight <60 kg (increased bleeding risk), active bleeding, pending urgent CABG (hold 7 days)
tooltip: Most potent thienopyridine P2Y12 blocker — faster, more complete than clopidogrel. Not dependent on CYP2C19. More bleeding risk. ABSOLUTE contraindication: prior stroke/TIA (net harm). Relative: age >75, weight <60 kg. Use in PCI-managed ACS when patient is suitable.

name: Bivalirudin
primaryEffect: Direct thrombin inhibition used as heparin alternative in PCI and HIT
treatmentUsage: PCI (as alternative to heparin — especially in HIT patients), HIT treatment, acute coronary syndrome
mechanismOfAction: Directly and reversibly inhibits thrombin (both clot-bound and free); does not require antithrombin III; bivalent inhibitor (blocks both active site and substrate recognition site of thrombin).
sideEffects: Bleeding, thrombocytopenia (rare), back pain
contraindications: Active major bleeding, severe renal failure (renally cleared)
tooltip: Direct thrombin inhibitor — used in PCI and as heparin alternative in HIT. Does not require AT-III. Bivalent (blocks both thrombin active site and substrate binding site). Short half-life. No reversal agent needed — clears quickly. Monitored by ACT during PCI.

name: Argatroban
primaryEffect: Hepatically cleared direct thrombin inhibitor — safe in renal failure
treatmentUsage: HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia) treatment, PCI in patients with HIT, renal failure patients needing anticoagulation
mechanismOfAction: Directly and reversibly inhibits thrombin at its active site (univalent inhibitor); hepatically metabolized — dose must be reduced in hepatic failure but safe in renal failure.
sideEffects: Bleeding, elevated INR (complicates bridging to warfarin — use chromogenic factor X assay instead), hepatic accumulation in liver disease
contraindications: Active major bleeding, severe hepatic impairment
tooltip: Direct thrombin inhibitor — first-line for HIT (along with bivalirudin). Hepatically cleared (safe in renal failure — use when fondaparinux or bivalirudin not available). Falsely elevates INR — use chromogenic factor X assay to monitor warfarin transition.

name: Fondaparinux
primaryEffect: Selective factor Xa inhibition via antithrombin; does NOT cause HIT
treatmentUsage: VTE treatment and prophylaxis, DVT/PE, NSTEMI/ACS (preferred in some settings), HIT (alternative anticoagulant — does NOT cross-react with HIT antibodies)
mechanismOfAction: Synthetic pentasaccharide that binds antithrombin III and dramatically enhances its inhibition of factor Xa specifically; does not bind PF4 and therefore does NOT cause HIT.
sideEffects: Bleeding, thrombocytopenia (rare — NOT HIT), injection site reactions, renally cleared
contraindications: Severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min — renally cleared), bacterial endocarditis
tooltip: Synthetic pentasaccharide — selective factor Xa inhibitor via AT-III. Does NOT cause HIT (does not bind PF4). Renally cleared — avoid in severe CKD. No reversal agent available (andexanet alfa works in theory). Used as alternative anticoagulant in HIT patients.

name: Streptokinase
primaryEffect: Non-fibrin-selective thrombolysis via indirect plasminogen activation
treatmentUsage: STEMI (when alteplase unavailable), DVT/PE, peripheral arterial occlusion; rarely used in developed countries due to immunogenicity
mechanismOfAction: Bacterial protein that forms a complex with plasminogen, converting other plasminogen molecules to plasmin; non-selective (systemic fibrinolysis, not fibrin-targeted) — greater bleeding risk.
sideEffects: Bleeding (significant — non-selective fibrinolysis), severe allergic reactions/anaphylaxis (bacterial origin — pre-treat with steroids/antihistamines), hypotension, antibody formation (cannot be reused within 6-12 months)
contraindications: Prior streptokinase treatment within 6-12 months (antibodies reduce efficacy and increase reactions), prior streptococcal infection (antibodies), all standard thrombolytic contraindications
tooltip: Non-fibrin-selective thrombolytic — forms plasminogen activator complex indirectly. More bleeding risk and allergic reactions than tPA. Cannot be reused (antibody formation). Hypotension common. Largely replaced by alteplase. Used where cost is limiting factor.
